# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ: Світ прикметників — Introduction: The World of Adjectives

Welcome to your first step into the world of Ukrainian adjectives — **прикметники** (adjectives)! You already know the three-gender system from the Gender Code module: masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns. Now you'll discover how adjectives change their endings to match the noun they describe.

In Ukrainian, every adjective answers one of four questions depending on the noun's gender and number:

- **Який?** (which? / what kind?) — for masculine nouns
- **Яка?** — for feminine nouns
- **Яке?** — for neuter nouns
- **Які?** — for plural nouns

Let's see this in action right away. Look at the word **рідний** (native, dear). Watch how it transforms:

- **рідний край** — native land (masculine)
- **рідна мова** — native language (feminine)
- **рідне слово** — native word (neuter)
- **рідні люди** — dear people (plural)

<!-- adapted from: Вашуленко, Grade 3 -->

One root — **рідн-** — and four different endings! This is the heart of adjective agreement in Ukrainian. You'll master this pattern by the end of this module.

> [!culture] **Софійський собор** (St. Sophia's Cathedral) in Kyiv is one of Ukraine's most treasured landmarks. Ukrainians describe it as **старий** (ancient) and **великий** (grand). To talk about Ukraine's history and culture, you need descriptive words — **прикметники**. By the end of this module, you'll be able to describe anything around you in Ukrainian!

You already know three genders from the Gender Code. Now you'll see how adjectives follow the same gender system. Think of it this way: the noun is the boss, and the adjective is the loyal assistant — it always matches the boss's gender. Get the noun's gender right, and the adjective ending follows automatically.

## Презентація: Тверда група — Presentation: Hard Stem Adjectives

Most Ukrainian adjectives belong to the **тверда група** (hard group). These adjectives have stems ending in a hard consonant, and their endings follow a clear, predictable pattern:

| Gender | Question | Ending | Example |
|--------|----------|--------|---------|
| Masculine 🔵 | Який? | **-ий** | **новий дім** (new house) |
| Feminine 🔴 | Яка? | **-а** | **нова книга** (new book) |
| Neuter 🟡 | Яке? | **-е** | **нове місто** (new city) |
| Plural 🟢 | Які? | **-і** | **нові друзі** (new friends) |

<!-- adapted from: Вашуленко, Grade 3, p. 130 -->

Let's practice with several high-frequency adjectives. Notice how the ending always follows the noun's gender:

**гарний** (beautiful, nice):
- **гарний день** — a beautiful day
- **гарна погода** — nice weather
- **гарне небо** — a beautiful sky
- **гарні дні** — beautiful days

**великий** (big, grand) and **малий** (small):
- **великий будинок** — a big building
- **мала кімната** — a small room
- **велике вікно** — a big window
- **малі двері** — small doors

### Adjective Position

In Ukrainian, adjectives usually come before the noun: **гарна погода** (nice weather). This is the most common pattern. But you can also place the adjective after the noun using the zero copula you already know:

- **Це гарна погода.** — This is nice weather.
- **Погода гарна.** — The weather is nice.

In both positions, the ending stays the same. You don't need to worry about the position changing anything — just match the gender!

> [!warning] **Common Mistake: Gender Mismatch**
> English doesn't have gender agreement, so beginners often use the masculine dictionary form (-ий) for everything. Watch out for this trap:
> - ❌ *новий машина* — wrong! **машина** is feminine
> - ✅ **нова машина** — a new car
>
> Always check the noun's gender first, then pick the matching adjective ending.

### Opposites — Your Descriptive Toolkit

Opposites are a powerful way to double your vocabulary quickly. Here are essential pairs you'll use every day:

**добрий / поганий** (good / bad):
- **добрий день** — good day
- **поганий настрій** — bad mood
- **добра людина** — a good person
- **погана погода** — bad weather

**великий / малий** (big / small):
- **великий дім** — a big house
- **малий дім** — a small house

**дорогий / дешевий** (expensive / cheap):
- **дорогий телефон** — an expensive phone
- **дешевий телефон** — a cheap phone

> [!tip] **Quick Check**: Before you add an adjective to a noun, ask yourself: «Який? Яка? Яке? Які?» The question tells you which ending to use!

## Презентація 2: М'яка група та Специфіка (Presentation 2: Soft Stem and Nuances)

Some adjectives belong to the **м'яка група** (soft group). Their stem ends in a soft consonant, and the endings look slightly different from the hard group:

| Gender | Question | Ending | Example |
|--------|----------|--------|---------|
| Masculine 🔵 | Який? | **-ій** | **синій** (blue) |
| Feminine 🔴 | Яка? | **-я** | **синя лінія** (blue line) |
| Neuter 🟡 | Яке? | **-є** | **синє небо** (blue sky) |
| Plural 🟢 | Які? | **-і** | **сині** (blue — plural) |

The word **синій** (blue) is your key example for the soft group. Here's a practical way to remember it: the Kyiv Metro has a **синя лінія** (Blue Line) and a **червона лінія** (Red Line). Notice: **синя** (soft, -я) vs. **червона** (hard, -а) — two different groups describing the same noun **лінія**!

> [!warning] **Watch the Spelling!**
> A common error is writing *синий* instead of the correct **синій**. Notice the difference carefully: the soft group masculine ending is **-ій** (with **ї**), not **-ий** (with **и**). This one letter makes all the difference between correct and incorrect Ukrainian.

Here's a side-by-side comparison of hard and soft endings:

| | Hard: **новий** | Soft: **синій** |
|---|---|---|
| Masculine | нов-**ий** | син-**ій** |
| Feminine | нов-**а** | син-**я** |
| Neuter | нов-**е** | син-**є** |
| Plural | нов-**і** | син-**і** |

### Plural — One Ending for All

Here's great news for you: in the plural, both hard and soft adjectives take the same ending — **-і**. And this ending is the same regardless of the noun's gender:

- **гарні дні** — beautiful days (masculine noun)
- **гарні дівчата** — beautiful girls (feminine noun)
- **гарні вікна** — beautiful windows (neuter noun)
- **сині олівці** — blue pencils (masculine noun)

No gender-matching needed in the plural — it's always **-і**! That's one less thing to worry about.

> [!note] **Hard vs. Soft — Quick Summary**
> - Hard group (most adjectives): **-ий, -а, -е, -і**
> - Soft group (like синій): **-ій, -я, -є, -і**
> - Plural is the same for both: always **-і**!

## Практика та Культурний контекст (Practice and Cultural Context)

### Культурний портрет: Мавка — Cultural Portrait: Mavka

**Мавка** is a forest spirit from Lesya Ukrainka's famous play «Лісова пісня» (Forest Song). She's one of the most beloved figures in Ukrainian literature — and she's perfect for practicing feminine adjective forms! Let's describe her:

- **Мавка — молода.** — Mavka is young.
- **Мавка — гарна.** — Mavka is beautiful.
- **Мавка — цікава.** — Mavka is interesting.
- **Це зелена Мавка.** — This is green Mavka.

Every adjective takes the feminine ending (-а/-я) because **Мавка** is a feminine noun. You're already using adjective agreement naturally!

### Агентство нерухомості — Real Estate Practice

Now imagine you're describing apartments and houses. All three genders appear naturally when talking about a home:

> **(Агентство нерухомості / Real Estate Agency)**

- **Це новий будинок.** — This is a new building.
- **Це мала квартира.** — This is a small apartment.
- **Це велике вікно.** — This is a big window.
- **Це дешева квартира.** — This is a cheap apartment.
- **Це дорогий дім.** — This is an expensive house.
- **Це стара лампа.** — This is an old lamp.

And for food in your new kitchen: **смачна каша** (tasty porridge), **смачний борщ** (tasty borshch), **смачне яблуко** (a tasty apple). The adjective **смачний** (tasty) works beautifully across all three genders!

> [!practice] **Your Turn!** Look around your room right now. Pick three objects you know in Ukrainian and describe each one with an adjective. Remember the steps: check the noun's gender → ask the matching question (Який? Яка? Яке?) → use the correct ending!

### The Який Pattern — Final Synthesis

Here's your complete question-and-answer toolkit for describing things:

- **Який дім? — Новий дім.** (What kind of house? — A new house.)
- **Яка книга? — Цікава книга.** (What kind of book? — An interesting book.)
- **Яке яблуко? — Смачне яблуко.** (What kind of apple? — A tasty apple.)
- **Які друзі? — Добрі друзі.** (What kind of friends? — Good friends.)

This question-answer pattern works for every adjective you'll ever learn. Master it now, and you'll be ready for the color vocabulary coming in the next module!

## Підсумок — Summary

You've taken a huge step in your Ukrainian journey! You now know how to describe people, places, and things using adjectives with correct gender agreement. Here's what you can do:

- Ask **який?/яка?/яке?/які?** to determine the correct adjective ending
- Apply hard-stem endings: **-ий, -а, -е, -і**
- Apply soft-stem endings: **-ій, -я, -є, -і**
- Use opposites like **великий/малий**, **добрий/поганий**, and **дорогий/дешевий** to describe your world
- Remember that plural adjectives always end in **-і**, regardless of gender

**Self-check questions:**
1. What ending does a hard-stem adjective take with a feminine noun?
2. How does **синій** change for a neuter noun?
3. What is the plural adjective ending — and does it differ between genders?
4. Why is *новий машина* incorrect?

## Вправи — Activities

Practice makes perfect! In these activities, you will focus on matching the correct adjective endings to nouns of different genders and numbers. Pay close attention to the vowel at the end of the noun to identify if it is masculine (consonant), feminine (-а/-я), or neuter (-о/-е). Remember: adjectives are the "mirrors" of nouns — they must reflect their partner's gender!

Вправи доступні в мобільному додатку та на платформі.

# Vocabulary

| Word | English | POS | Gender | Note |
|------|---------|-----|--------|------|
| новий | new | adj | m | hard stem |
| старий | old | adj | m | hard stem |
| гарний | beautiful/nice | adj | m | hard stem |
| великий | big/large | adj | m | hard stem |
| малий | small | adj | m | hard stem |
| добрий | good/kind | adj | m | hard stem |
| поганий | bad | adj | m | hard stem |
| цікавий | interesting | adj | m | hard stem |
| синій | blue | adj | m | soft stem |
| смачний | tasty | adj | m | hard stem |

In the next module, you'll learn color vocabulary and describe clothing — your adjective agreement skills will be essential! Keep practicing, and you'll be describing the world in Ukrainian in no time.
```

    - молоде
    - молоді

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Describing Things - Adjectives

## State Standard Reference
§4.2.1.2: "Adjective declension — hard/soft groups, gender agreement" (lines 633–650, A1 morphology section)
Alignment: This module directly delivers the §4.2.1.2 requirement — Nominative-case adjective agreement across masculine, feminine, neuter, and plural in both hard and soft stem groups.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

| Word | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-------|------------------|
| новий | Top-100 adjective; high-frequency, appears across all registers | новий рік, нове місто, нова книга |
| старий | Double meaning: old (age) + ancient (buildings) | старий будинок, стара книга, стара людина |
| гарний | Most natural Ukrainian beauty/nice adjective; NOT красивий at A1 | гарна погода, гарний день, гарне місто |
| великий | Scale & grandeur; appears in fixed phrases | велике місто, великий привіт, велика родина |
| малий | Primary antonym of великий; also means "young child" in some contexts | мале вікно, малий бізнес |
| добрий | Greetings function (добрий день) AND moral quality | добрий день, добра людина, добре серце |
| поганий | Primary negative descriptor; not рus. плохой | погана погода, поганий настрій |
| цікавий | Personal expression staple | цікава книга, цікаве питання |
| синій | Key soft-stem exemplar (-ій); distinguishes hard vs soft | синя лінія, синє небо, синій олівець |
| червоний | Hard-stem color; used for contrastive pair with синій | червона лінія, червоне яблуко |
| смачний | Food descriptor; high A1 relevance | смачна страва, смачний борщ |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Textbook discovery hook — рідний paradigm**: The Vashulenko Grade 3 textbook uses *«рідний край / рідна мова / рідне слово / рідні люди»* to introduce adjective gender agreement — one adjective, all four forms. This is a semantically rich anchor (homeland, language, native people) and works perfectly as a discovery warm-up. Students see the pattern before the rule is stated.

2. **Kyiv Metro lines**: «синя лінія» (Blue Line M1) and «червона лінія» (Red Line M2) — real, Google-verifiable transport data. This anchors soft-stem синій vs. hard-stem червоний in functional context. Learners can look it up on their phone.

3. **Mavka (Мавка) from Lesya Ukrainka's "Forest Song" (Лісова пісня)**: A молода, гарна, зелена forest spirit — all feminine forms. This gives the feminine paradigm a memorable cultural persona. Lesya Ukrainka is printed on the 200-hryvnia note, so it's also a practical touchpoint.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Gender mismatch — masculine dictionary form applied universally**: *\*новий машина* → correct: **нова машина**. English has no gender, so learners default to the dictionary form (-ий). Textbook Grade 3 Kravtsova explicitly targets this with fill-in exercises where gender must be determined from the noun.

2. **Hard/soft confusion — \*синий vs. синій**: English phonetics push learners toward -ий spelling since the word sounds similar to "siniy." The two-dot Ukrainian letter й after и must be drilled. Textbook Grade 4 Kravtsova shows color adjectives in context; use the Metro mnemonic to fix this.

3. **Plural form — gender-differentiated endings**: Learners try *\*гарна дні / гарне дівчата* — they carry singular gender logic into the plural. Ukrainian plural adjective form is always -і regardless of noun gender: **гарні дні, гарні дівчата, гарні вікна**.

4. **Adjective after noun in predicative position**: English "the house is big" maps naturally to Ukrainian «будинок великий» — acceptable but less natural than «великий будинок». At A1 this is secondary; priority is getting the ending correct. Note: verbs are forbidden at M11, so predicative sentences with є cannot be taught here.

---

## Cross-References

- **Builds on**: a1-07 (The Gender Code) — noun genders are the prerequisite; without knowing a noun's gender, adjective ending cannot be selected. No other prerequisites needed.
- **Prepares for**: a1-12 (Colors and Clothing) — expands adjective vocabulary with color terms; a1-42 (Description: Adverbs) — adverbs like гарно, добре derive from these same adjectives.

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise** (from Vashulenko Grade 3 model): Present four noun phrases without explanation — *рідний край, рідна мова, рідне слово, рідні люди* — and ask: "What changes? What stays the same?" Learners notice the shared root (рідн-) and the changing endings. Then show the question patterns: «Який? → -ий / Яка? → -а / Яке? → -е / Які? → -і». Rule emerges from data.

- **Best context**: Real estate / apartment listing. "Це новий будинок. Це мала квартира. Це велике вікно." — all three genders appear naturally in describing one property. The plan's Real Estate Agent persona fits well here.

- **Contrastive pairs** for disambiguation:
  - Hard vs. soft: **новий** (hard, -ий) vs. **синій** (soft, -ій) — same question «Який?», different ending
  - Singular vs. plural: **гарний день** vs. **гарні дні** — plural always -і
  - Masc vs. Fem: **новий рік** (m) vs. **нова квартира** (f) — use color-coding or table

---

## Notes for Content Writing

- Module M11 is pre-verbal: NO conjugated verbs, NO imperatives. All Ukrainian sentences must be noun phrases or «Це + adj + noun» patterns. Classroom instructions stay in English.
- The plan's section 2 mentions predicative position with «бути» — this is **not allowed at M11** since бути is a verb. Reframe predicative as «Це + noun» only: «Це нова квартира» (not «Квартира є нова»).
- Bilingual section headers required (M1-14 style): e.g., *«## Тверда група — Hard Stem Adjectives»*
- Hard-stem paradigm table (4 forms) and soft-stem paradigm table (4 forms) should be visual and color-coded — this is how Ukrainian school textbooks Grade 3-4 present it.
- Do NOT use the word «прикметник» in Ukrainian without English gloss — learners haven't seen Ukrainian grammatical terminology yet.
- Grade 5 Avramenko excerpt shows that description/adjective vocabulary directly enables portrait-writing — useful framing for the Mavka practice section.

---

## Friction Constraints (Past Review Findings)

FRICTION CONSTRAINTS (from past build reviews — DO NOT repeat these errors):
- [GLOBAL] сес-тра is a VALID word division per Правопис 2019 §49. Do NOT mark it as an error. Phonetic syllabification (се-стра) and typographic word division (сес-тра) follow different rules — both are correct in their respective contexts.
- [GLOBAL] Ukrainian textbooks teach a hands-on-EARS test for voicing (закрий долонями вуха), NOT a hand-on-throat test. The hand-on-throat test is a valid phonetics technique but must NOT be attributed to Ukrainian textbooks. Source: Кравцова 2019, Grade 2, p.39.
- [GLOBAL] Do NOT invent Ukrainian words for minimal pairs. "Сір" is NOT a word meaning "grey" — the correct form is "сірий". Use verified minimal pairs only: кит/кіт, бити/біти, лис/ліс.

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/describing-things-adjectives.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
